title: Helvetica Identity System
description: A stripped-back rebrand that puts typography at the centre of everything — no logomark, no colour system, just letterforms.
body:
Studio Nord was commissioned to rethink the visual identity of a century-old Swiss typefoundry. The brief was simple: trust the type. The result is an identity system built entirely around Helvetica Neue in its six canonical weights, applied across every touchpoint from signage to digital.

The colour palette is monochromatic — near-black on white, white on near-black. Paper choices do the heavy lifting where materials are involved. The system scales from a business card to a 12-metre facade with zero degradation.

This project won a D&AD Pencil in Brand Identity and was featured in Eye Magazine Issue 118.

title: Atlas Studio Rebrand
description: A full identity overhaul for a multidisciplinary architecture practice, grounded in cartographic visual language.
body:
Atlas Studio approached Pentagram with a challenge: their old brand felt like a construction company, not a creative practice. The new identity draws from vintage cartographic conventions — hairline grids, contour-line textures, and a logotype set in a modified version of Baskerville.

The wordmark is paired with a bespoke icon system that maps each service area to a different topographic symbol. Applied across stationery, wayfinding, and digital, the system is warm and rigorous in equal measure.

title: Nomad Brand Language
description: A fluid identity system for a travel platform built around the idea that place shapes identity.
body:
Nomad asked for a brand that could feel at home anywhere in the world without resorting to generic globe iconography. The solution is a colour system derived from satellite photography — each destination generates its own palette from its landscape.

The logotype is set in a custom-drawn sans that references international road signage, while the editorial layout system uses asymmetric grids borrowed from European modernism.

title: Atlas Studio Website
description: A portfolio site that uses scroll-driven animation to turn project browsing into a cinematic experience.
body:
Instrument built Atlas's new web presence around a single editorial constraint: every project should feel like opening a magazine spread. Scroll-driven animations reveal imagery at 24fps, mimicking film. The type is set at editorial scale — some headings exceed 200px — and the navigation is buried until needed.

Built in Next.js with custom WebGL transitions between projects, the site loads in under 1.8 seconds on a mid-range connection despite its visual ambition.

title: Tempo Dashboard
description: A time-tracking SaaS redesign that reduces cognitive load through radical information hierarchy.
body:
Tempo's original dashboard surfaced 47 data points on the default view. The redesign reduces that to 6, with everything else accessible in one additional click. The visual language borrows from analogue instruments — gauges, dials, and progress arcs — to make abstract time data feel tangible.

The component system was built in Figma and shipped as a custom design token set compatible with the engineering team's existing Tailwind setup.

title: Bloom Portfolio System
description: An open-source portfolio template system built for creative professionals who want full typographic control.
body:
Bloom started as a personal project and became a community resource. The system ships with 12 layout presets, a complete variable font stack, and a dark-mode-first colour system that adapts to the user's OS preference.

Each layout preset is built on a shared 12-column grid with variable gutter and margin tokens, making it easy to switch between editorial, agency, and studio aesthetics without touching the underlying CSS.

title: Sequence 01 Showreel
description: A 90-second motion showreel exploring the relationship between typographic rhythm and sound design.
body:
ManvsMachine director Riku Mäkinen spent six months developing Sequence 01 as an experimental piece outside of client work. Every frame is generated from the same underlying grid, with letterforms growing, collapsing, and re-forming in response to a custom score composed by Oslo-based musician Tor Haugen.

The piece won Gold at the Motionographer Awards 2025 and has been selected for the OFFF Barcelona permanent archive.

title: Kinetic Type Experiment
description: A browser-based experiment where variable fonts respond to cursor velocity and ambient sound input.
body:
Built entirely in the browser with CSS Houdini and the Web Audio API, this experiment asks what happens when type becomes truly responsive — not to screen size, but to human behaviour. Move fast and the letterforms stretch thin; slow down and they settle into weight.

The piece runs at 60fps on any modern browser without a GPU and has been adopted by several type foundries as an interactive specimen format.

title: FORM Annual Report 2025
description: A 220-page annual report that treats financial disclosure as a design opportunity, not a compliance obligation.
body:
Design Bridge were commissioned to rethink FORM's annual report from first principles. The result is a document that reads more like a catalogue than a regulatory filing. Financial tables are broken up with full-bleed photography, pullquotes are set at 72pt, and each section opens with a hand-lettered chapter title.

Printed on four paper stocks with two spot colours, the report won the Cannes Lions Grand Prix for Print & Publishing.

title: Grain Magazine — Issue 12
description: An independent food and culture publication known for its unconventional grid and refusal to use white space conventionally.
body:
Grain is independently published quarterly by Nadia Petrov from her studio in Vilnius. Issue 12, themed around fermentation, features a deliberately uncomfortable layout — text overruns images, columns bleed into the gutter, and the cover type is set upside-down.

The publication is printed in a limited run of 800 on uncoated stock and has a three-issue waiting list.

title: FORM Chair Collection
description: A family of three chairs designed around a single aluminium extrusion profile, assembled without tools.
body:
Leo Marchetti's FORM collection for Hay explores what happens when a single manufacturing constraint — one aluminium profile, extruded in three lengths — becomes the generative rule for an entire furniture family. The lounge, dining, and stool variants share the same legs, connectors, and seat attachment method.

The design language references Scandinavian rationalism without replicating its forms. The collection launched at Salone del Mobile 2025 and entered production in September.

title: Lume Pendant Lamp
description: A pendant lamp that uses a single piece of spun aluminium to create both the shade and the diffuser.
body:
Soo-Jin Park's Lume pendant collapses the conventional lamp structure — shade, housing, diffuser, cord grip — into a single spun aluminium form. The shallow concave reflector distributes light across 160 degrees, eliminating the typical dark zone above the shade.

The lamp is available in four anodised finishes and ships flat-packed in a sleeve made from the same aluminium sheet offcut, reducing packaging waste by 60% versus the previous studio release.

title: Drift Watch Identity
description: A complete brand and packaging system for an independent watchmaker built around the concept of controlled imprecision.
body:
Koto developed Drift's brand around a single conceptual tension: the beauty of a watch that runs slightly wrong. The identity uses a custom typeface where every character is offset by two degrees — imperceptible at small sizes, striking at display scale.

Packaging is machined aluminium with a soft-close magnetic lid. The dial design, developed in collaboration with movement specialist Cécile Morin, uses a graduated chapter ring that subtly accelerates toward 12.

title: Mindful App Redesign
description: A meditation app redesigned around the insight that most wellness apps cause the anxiety they claim to treat.
body:
IDEO's research phase revealed that Mindful's original app was generating engagement anxiety — users felt guilty when they missed sessions and compulsively checked streak counts. The redesign removes streaks, removes push notifications by default, and replaces the dashboard with a single question: how are you right now?

The visual language is deliberately low-stimulus: no gradients, no animation except breath-paced transitions, and a type-only approach that removes all decorative illustration.

title: Wayfinder Maps
description: A pedestrian navigation app that replaces turn-by-turn instructions with neighbourhood-scale spatial awareness.
body:
Stamen Design's Wayfinder project started from a frustration: existing navigation apps make you a passive passenger in your own city. Wayfinder instead shows you the district you're in, the one you're heading toward, and landmarks within a five-minute walk — no arrow, no countdown.

The map rendering uses a custom cartographic style derived from 1970s transit maps, with neighbourhood names at typographic scale and building footprints shown only in the immediate vicinity.

title: Vault Finance App
description: A personal finance app that treats money management as a long-term practice, not a daily anxiety loop.
body:
The Figma Design team built Vault as a reference implementation for the new Figma Variables system, but it became something more: a considered critique of fintech UX conventions. There are no red numbers, no alert banners, and no 'you spent 40% more than last month' shame mechanics.

Instead, Vault uses a long-scroll timeline that shows financial patterns over months and years, with a forecasting view that treats projections as ranges, not false precision.
